text: The surgeon begins the procedure by carefully identifying the course of the round ligament, which is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for adhesions that may distort the region around the round ligament during the laparoscopic groin hernia repair.
The surgeon identifies a femoral defect alongside the right external iliac vein, medial ligament, and round ligament of the uterus during the laparoscopic groin hernia repair.
The surgeon visualizes the medial border of the external iliac vein and its intersection with the round ligament during the laparoscopic groin hernia repair.
The surgeon performs minimal dissection of the peritoneum flap, moving it downwards to expose underlying structures.
The round ligament is visibly thicker as it enters the uterine horn, making dissection challenging and prone to bleeding in this area.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum and begins dissection of the preperitoneal space, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies Cooper's ligament and the femoral defect, which has a narrow orifice and is surrounded by dense adhesions.

The surgeon reduces the hernia content after widening the orifice and clears the defect margins. A lower peritoneal flap is formed to create space for a synthetic mesh, ensuring the flap is wide enough to cover the mesh without lifting it. In females, the round ligament of the uterus is dissected or transected to achieve sufficient margin downwards.
The surgeon gently sweeps the round ligament off the peritoneum after releasing minor adhesions, ensuring it does not adhere tightly to the peritoneal surface.
During the dissection near the uterine horn, the surgeon observes the decreasing distance between the round ligament and the peritoneum, indicating a challenging area for further dissection.

The surgeon attempts to separate the ligament from the peritoneum using scissors and coagulation, but due to the thinning of the peritoneum, switches to blunt dissection to carefully separate the structures.
As the surgeon applies the technique, minor bleeding is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses coagulation to control minor bleeding during the dissection of the round ligament of the uterus, ensuring the surgical field remains clear.
The surgeon carefully sweeps the intact peritoneum off the ligament, maintaining the separation between these structures during the dissection.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection of the peritoneum, encountering minor bleeding which is controlled with coagulation. Upon inspecting the lifted peritoneum, a small tear in the peritoneal flap is visible.

The surgeon observes a small rent in the peritoneum from the upper side, which is visible during the dissection. This opening facilitates easier dissection, allowing better control over the ligament and uterus position.
The surgeon continues the dissection despite an incidental opening in the peritoneum, successfully releasing the round ligament.
The surgeon places a mesh over the hernia site, ensuring that the lower margin of the mesh does not interfere with the round ligament.
The surgeon secures the mesh to Cooper's ligament using a single suture, ensuring the mesh is firmly anchored in place.
The surgeon leaves a needle with a suture inside the peritoneal cavity, visible for later use in the procedure.

The surgeon closes the peritoneum tightly using a V-lock suture, ensuring the mesh is separated from the peritoneal cavity, although there remains potential contact at the proximal part of the round ligament.
The surgeon grasps the stock needle and begins closing the defect located just above the area where the peritoneum was torn from the round ligament.
The surgeon applies a single Z-type suture to tightly close the peritoneum, ensuring there is no visible risk of internal herniation of the bowel into the preperitoneal space or mesh.

The surgeon performs a final inspection, confirming the full closure of the peritoneum and the proper positioning of the mesh without any folding, ensuring no risk of internal herniation at the sutured locations.
The round ligament is clearly visible beneath the transparent peritoneum, indicating its anatomical position in relation to the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the preperitoneal space and identifies the Cooper's ligament, then begins dissecting the hernia sac from the inguinal canal.
The surgeon carefully sweeps the round ligament, ensuring not to damage the surrounding peritoneum during the procedure.
The surgeon proceeds with the transection well below the hernia orifice, ensuring careful separation from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum from the round ligament, observing that the ligament appears thicker and more fragile, while the peritoneum becomes visibly thinner during the dissection.
The surgeon observes that the hernia sac and surrounding tissue structures have adhered tightly, appearing as if they are merged into a single entity.
The surgeon decides to abandon further stripping of the peritoneum from the round ligament due to the potential for troublesome bleeding.
The surgeon divides the peritoneum parallel to the medial border of the ligament, extending the incision down towards the uterine horn.
The dissection line is visibly close to the uterus, highlighting the proximity of the surgical action to this anatomical structure.
The surgeon performs a similar maneuver on the lateral side of the round ligament, using scissors to incise the peritoneum downwards to match the level of the incision on the medial side.
The surgeon ensures adequate mesh overlap by dissecting beyond the medial border of the external iliac vein.
The surgeon inserts and unfolds the mesh within the surgical field, ensuring it covers the dissected area adequately.
The lower margin of the mesh is positioned without touching the edge of the dissected peritoneal flap, indicating careful placement within the surgical field.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum incision using an absorbable V-lock suture, leaving the excessive thread uncut after completing the suturing process.
The surgeon invaginates the hernia sac and identifies two visible defects in the peritoneum.
The surgeon uses a few suture passes between the peritoneum and hernia sac to close the smaller defect, placate the hernia sac, and reach the apex of the lower defect.
The surgeon closes the rent tightly, ensuring there is no visible gap that could lead to internal herniation.
The surgeon locks the suture, completing the closure of the peritoneal incision with absorbable V-lock suture, leaving the excessive thread uncut.
The surgeon inserts a suction tube beyond the peritoneum and performs gas deflation, visually confirming the appropriate closure and positioning of the mesh.
During the laparoscopic groin hernia repair, the surgeon transsects the round ligament of the uterus, a common step in this procedure.
